headline: New Hampshire Town Builds Tunnels to Save Endangered Salamanders
meta_description: Keene, New Hampshire is constructing underground passages to help imperiled Jefferson complex salamanders safely cross roads during migration season.
summary_short: Keene, New Hampshire is building tunnels under Jordan Road to protect hybrid Jefferson/blue-spotted salamanders from traffic during spring migration.
body:
Every spring, thousands of amphibians in Keene, New Hampshire face a deadly obstacle: a two-lane asphalt road blocking their journey from winter forests to breeding wetlands. Now the town is taking action by constructing small tunnels beneath Jordan Road—a crossing site home to Jefferson complex salamanders, a hybrid species classified by the state as most in need of conservation. The underpasses, designed to guide animals safely underground with the help of protective fencing on both sides of the road, represent a shift from hand-carrying individual creatures across traffic. If construction begins as planned next summer, the tunnels should be fully operational in time for the spring 2028 migration season.

This initiative draws inspiration from proven success nearby. In 2015, the Vermont town of Monkton built similar culvert underpasses that reduced amphibian deaths on their road by 80 percent—a dramatic improvement documented in a 2024 study. That project emerged after a devastating 2006 survey recorded over 1,000 amphibians killed trying to cross Monkton Road in just two nights. Habitat fragmentation caused by roads is overwhelmingly the greatest threat to New England amphibians, experts say, forcing creatures to navigate dangerous passages to find food, mates, and shelter across a landscape increasingly carved up by human development.

The Keene project is a collaboration between the Harris Center for Conservation Education, the city, and New Hampshire Fish and Game Department, funded by a grant from the National Fish and Wildlife Foundation. This year alone, volunteer brigades counted 108 Jefferson complex salamanders at Jordan Road—nearly double any previous season—underscoring both the species' presence at the site and the dedication of those who've walked dark, rainy nights with flashlights to safely guide these imperiled creatures across asphalt.
quick_facts: Keene, New Hampshire building tunnels under Jordan Road for salamanders
Jefferson complex salamanders classified as most in need of conservation
Vermont's similar 2015 project reduced amphibian deaths by 80 percent
Tunnels expected operational by spring 2028 migration season
Over 1,000 amphibians killed on one Vermont road in two nights, 2006
